title: Multidimensional Biodiversity Index (MBI)
text:
Biodiversity indexes are mathematical measures used by ecologists to quantify the variety of life in an ecosystem.
Here we develop a new approach to calculate three biodiversity indexes that will be combined as the Multidimensional Biodiversity Index (MBI) at the end.


Codelibrary(grateful) # Facilitate Citation of R Packages
library(rmarkdown) # Dynamic Documents for R
library(readxl) # Read Excel Files
library(DT) # A Wrapper of the JavaScript Library 'DataTables'
library(sf) # Simple Features for R
library(mapview) # Interactive Viewing of Spatial Data in R
library(maps) # Draw Geographical Maps
library(tmap) # Thematic Maps
library(beepr) # Easily Play Notification Sounds on any Platform 
library(spOccupancy) # Single-Species, Multi-Species, and Integrated Spatial Occupancy Models
library(posterior) # Tools for Working with Posterior Distributions
library(fundiversity) # Easy Computation of Functional Diversity Indices
library(boot) # Bootstrap Functions (Originally by Angelo Canty for S)
library(downlit) # Syntax Highlighting and Automatic Linking

library(abind) # Combine Multidimensional Arrays 
library(patchwork) # The Composer of Plots
library(boot) # Bootstrap Functions (Originally by Angelo Canty for S) 
library(tidyverse) # Easily Install and Load the 'Tidyverse'
library(plyr) # Tools for Splitting, Applying and Combining Data
library(scales) # Scale Functions for Visualization
library(reshape2) # Flexibly Reshape Data: A Reboot of the Reshape Package
library(plotly) # Create Interactive Web Graphics via 'plotly.js'



Using acoustic sensors a total of 135 species were detected across the 78 sites, including 119 birds, 4 mammals, 11 amphibians, and 1 reptile, with seven bird species currently listed as threatened on the IUCN Red List. Invertivores and omnivores accounted for over 70% of detected bird species, with nearly half associated with semi-open or open habitats, reflecting a dominance of generalist and disturbance-tolerant species The best model was a spatial model including mean canopy cover and habitat type (rubber management systems and forest areas). We ran the spatial model with mammals, birds and amphibians Canopy Cover: Mean canopy cover had a weak and non-significant effect on community occupancy Monoculture was used as the baseline (intercept). Species occupancy was significantly higher in advanced Wanakaset sites compared to monocultures. Forest and early Wanakaset areas showed high internal variability and did not differ significantly from monocultures. Estimated species richness was highest in advanced Wanakaset sites, followed by early-stage Wanakaset and monoculture areas.

title: Multidimensional Biodiversity Index (MBI)
text:
Biodiversity indexes are mathematical measures used by ecologists to quantify the variety of life in an ecosystem.
Here we develop a new approach to calculate three biodiversity indexes that will be combined as the Multidimensional Biodiversity Index (MBI) at the end.


Codelibrary(grateful) # Facilitate Citation of R Packages
library(rmarkdown) # Dynamic Documents for R
library(readxl) # Read Excel Files
library(DT) # A Wrapper of the JavaScript Library 'DataTables'
library(sf) # Simple Features for R
library(mapview) # Interactive Viewing of Spatial Data in R
library(maps) # Draw Geographical Maps
library(tmap) # Thematic Maps
library(beepr) # Easily Play Notification Sounds on any Platform

library(posterior) # Tools for Working with Posterior Distributions
library(fundiversity) # Easy Computation of Functional Diversity Indices

library(plotly) # Create Interactive Web Graphics via 'plotly.js'



Using acoustic sensors a total of 135 species were detected across the 78 sites, including 119 birds, 4 mammals, 11 amphibians, and 1 reptile, with seven bird species currently listed as threatened on the IUCN Red List. Invertivores and omnivores accounted for over 70% of detected bird species, with nearly half associated with semi-open or open habitats, reflecting a dominance of generalist and disturbance-tolerant species The best model was a spatial model including mean canopy cover and habitat type (rubber management systems and forest areas). We ran the spatial model with mammals, birds and amphibians Canopy Cover: Mean canopy cover had a weak and non-significant effect on community occupancy Monoculture was used as the baseline (intercept). Species occupancy was significantly higher in advanced Wanakaset sites compared to monocultures. Forest and early Wanakaset areas showed high internal variability and did not differ significantly from monocultures. Estimated species richness was highest in advanced Wanakaset sites, followed by early-stage Wanakaset and monoculture areas.

title: Multidimensional Biodiversity Index (MBI)
text:
Taxonomic Diversity (TD)
This is not just species richness. Here we calculated the TD as the geometric mean of occupancy for each species, representing the abundance of each species at the sampled places.
\[
TD_{t}=exp(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i}^{} ln \frac{d_{i,t}}{d_{i,1}})
\] were:
- n = number of species.
- di,t = abundance of species i at time t.
- di,1 = abundance of species i at time 1.
Since we have just one temporal measurement the base line to compare is TD at Monoculture.
The occupancy for each species was calculated using the package spOccupancy resulting as a table of mean occupancy of posteriors per site.
Load the data:

Code# Load occupancy

  mean_occupancy <- read_csv("C:/CodigoR/WildMon/FunctionalDiversityV3/data/psi_mean_THAILAND_VF.csv") |> 
    # rename(site_name = `site name`) |> 
    # mutate(site_name = str_replace_all(site_name, " ", "_")) |> 
    mutate(across(where(is.numeric), round, digits = 3))

names(mean_occupancy)[names(mean_occupancy) == "site name"] <- "site_name"



Codedatatable(mean_occupancy, options = list(pageLength = 10)) 






Codedf1 <- mean_occupancy [,-c(2,3)]
df<-melt(df1)
colnames(df)<-c("Sampled_site","Species","value")

# Plot heatmap
p <- ggplot(df, aes(Species, Sampled_site)) + 
  geom_tile(aes(fill = value),colour = "white") + 
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white",high = "#1E5A8C")+
  scale_x_discrete(expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_discrete(expand = c(0, 0)) + 
  theme(legend.position = "none",axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1, size=5, face = "italic"),
        axis.text.y = element_text(size=4))  +
  labs(title = "Species occupancy per sampled site")

# ggplotly(p) # see interactive
# View the plot
p







Calculating TD per grouping factor
The TD formula is the geometric mean of occupancy ratios relative to the baseline period \(\hat{\psi}_1\). Using model-estimated \(\hat{\psi}_t\)
\[TD_t = \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln\frac{\hat{\psi}_{i,t}}{\hat{\psi}_{i,1}}\right)\]

Code#' @param psi_t  Numeric vector: estimated occupancy at time t  (length n_sp)
#' @param psi_1  Numeric vector: estimated occupancy at baseline (length n_sp)
#' @return Scalar TD value (1 = no community-level change from baseline)

calc_TD <- function(psi_t, psi_1) {
  psi_t[psi_t <= 0] <- 1e-6
  psi_1[psi_1 <= 0] <- 1e-6
  exp(mean(log(psi_t / psi_1)))
}


To calculate TD per sampled site we use an R idiomatic and cleaner version:
TD_t <- exp(mean(log( x ))). And next we summarize by grouping factor.

Code  mean_occupancy$TD <-  df1[,-c(1)] |> 
  apply(MARGIN = 1, FUN = function(x) exp(mean(log(x))))  # function for TD
  
  group_summary_TD <-  mean_occupancy |> 
  # melt() |> 
    group_by(integration_class) %>%
    summarise(
      mean_TD = mean(TD, na.rm = TRUE),
      SD_TD = sd(TD, na.rm = TRUE),
      .groups = "drop"
    )


TD_t <- exp(mean(log(group_summary_TD$mean_TD / rep(group_summary_TD$mean_TD[4], 4) )))



Code  plot_data_group0 <- group_summary_TD |> 
    # filter(!is.na(mean_FDiv_obs)) %>%
    # arrange(mean_FDiv_obs) %>%
    mutate(group_factor = factor(integration_class, levels = integration_class))

TD_plot <- ggplot(plot_data_group0, aes(x = group_factor, y = mean_TD)) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_TD-SD_TD, ymax = mean_TD+SD_TD, color = "Standard deviation"),
      width = 0.3
    ) +
    geom_point(aes(color = "Observed TD"), size = 2) +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_color_manual(values = c(
      "Observed TD" = "gray25",
      "Standard deviation" = "#9987FF"
    )) +
    labs(
      title = "Taxonomic Diversity by Group",
      x = "Group", y = "Taxonomic Diversity"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")


TD_plot








Codedatatable(group_summary_TD)

title: Multidimensional Biodiversity Index (MBI)
text:
Functional Diversity (FD)






NoteFunctional Diversity (FD):



Should be calculated as Functional Divergence using the package fundiversity and the function fd_fdiv, since input values are continuous occupancy probabilities (ψ, 0–1), not binary presence absence.


Functional Divergence represents how abundance (occupancy) is spread along a functional trait axis, within the range occupied by the community. For instance, divergence is low when the most abundant species have functional traits that are close to the center of the functional trait range. Conversely, when the most abundant species have extreme functional trait values, then divergence is high. In a multivariate context, functional divergence relates to how abundance is distributed within the volume of functional trait space occupied by species.
Occupancy was already estimated using the package spOccupancy, and is comming from the posteriors located at a three dimensional array iter_sp_site where each species was detected in each site, corrected by detectability, using 3000 iterations. The psi values in the array iter_sp_site have 3000 iterations per species per site in that order. The psi values of the posteriors are used in the Functional Diversity formula and are provided by array psi_raw_THAILAND_VF.rds.
Calculating Functional Diversity requires species-level trait data. Traits for each species are provided by the table avonet_traits_birdlife.csv located at the data folder.
Functional Diversity uses four traits:

Wing Length.

Mass.

Habitat Density.

Trophic Level.

Functional Diversity comes without error or confidence intervals. So we need to implement a bootstrap and jackknife strategy to estimate error per site and per grouping factor using the posteriors (calculated using the spOccupancy package) as the re-sampling basis for psi. The grouping factors per site are located in the column integration_class of the table psi_mean_THAILAND_VF.csv saved at the data folder.
Next we load some helper functions to make the calculations.

Code#' Load and align trait and occupancy data
load_and_clean_data <- function() {
  # Use relative paths
  # Load occupancy, traits, and FHD stats
  site_comm_raw <- read_csv("C:/CodigoR/WildMon/FunctionalDiversityV3/data/psi_mean_THAILAND_VF.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
    rename(site_name = `site name`) %>%
    mutate(site_name = str_replace_all(site_name, " ", "_"))

  iter_sp_site <- readRDS("C:/CodigoR/WildMon/FunctionalDiversityV3/data/psi_raw_THAILAND_VF.rds")
  traits_raw <- read_csv("C:/CodigoR/WildMon/FunctionalDiversityV3/data/avonet_traits_birdlife.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)


  # Align species
  common_species <- intersect(colnames(site_comm_raw), traits_raw$Species1)


  # Select and format traits
  traits <- traits_raw %>%
    filter(Species1 %in% common_species) %>%
    select(Species1, Wing.Length, Mass, Habitat.Density, Trophic.Level) %>%
    mutate(across(c(Wing.Length, Mass, Habitat.Density, Trophic.Level), as.numeric)) %>%
    mutate(across(c(Wing.Length, Mass, Habitat.Density, Trophic.Level), ~ if_else(.x == 0, NA_real_, .x))) %>%
    mutate(across(c(Wing.Length, Mass, Habitat.Density, Trophic.Level), log)) %>%
    drop_na() %>%
    column_to_rownames("Species1")

  common_species <- rownames(traits)

  site_comm <- site_comm_raw %>%
    select(site_name, integration_class, all_of(common_species))

  # Transpose the array to have: species, sites, and iteration
  # The RDS is (iteration, species, site)
  sp_site_iter <- aperm(iter_sp_site, c(2, 3, 1))

  # Ensure dim names match
  dimnames(sp_site_iter) <- list(
    species = dimnames(iter_sp_site)[[2]],
    sites = dimnames(iter_sp_site)[[3]],
    iteration = NULL
  )

  return(list(traits = traits, site_comm = site_comm, sp_site_iter = sp_site_iter))
}


#' Estimate site-level uncertainty using posterior iterations and species jackknife
estimate_site_uncertainty <- function(obs_fdiv_df, traits, site_comm, sp_site_iter, common_species) {
  site_results <- obs_fdiv_df %>%
    left_join(site_comm %>% select(site_name, integration_class), by = "site_name") %>%
    mutate(
      post_mean = NA_real_,
      post_low = NA_real_,
      post_high = NA_real_,
      jack_low = NA_real_,
      jack_high = NA_real_
    )

  sites <- site_results$site_name
  all_posterior_sites <- dimnames(sp_site_iter)$sites

  for (i in seq_along(sites)) {
    s_name <- sites[i]

    if (!(s_name %in% all_posterior_sites)) next

    # 1. Posterior Uncertainty
    # Sample iterations
    iter_indices <- sample(1:dim(sp_site_iter)[3], N_ITER)
    # Subset psi for this site across sampled iterations
    site_psi_samples <- sp_site_iter[common_species, s_name, iter_indices] # species x iterations

    post_fdiv_vals <- apply(site_psi_samples, 2, function(psi_vec) {
      comm_mat <- matrix(psi_vec, nrow = 1)
      colnames(comm_mat) <- common_species
      rownames(comm_mat) <- "site"
      res <- tryCatch(fd_fdiv(traits, comm_mat), error = function(e) NULL)
      if (is.null(res)) {
        return(NA)
      }
      return(res$FDiv)
    })

    site_results$post_mean[i] <- mean(post_fdiv_vals, na.rm = TRUE)
    site_results$post_low[i] <- quantile(post_fdiv_vals, (1 - CONF_LEVEL) / 2, na.rm = TRUE)
    site_results$post_high[i] <- quantile(post_fdiv_vals, 1 - (1 - CONF_LEVEL) / 2, na.rm = TRUE)

    # 2. Species Jackknife (using mean psi)
    site_psi_mean <- site_comm %>%
      filter(site_name == s_name) %>%
      select(all_of(common_species)) %>%
      as.numeric()
    names(site_psi_mean) <- common_species

    jack_vals <- sapply(seq_along(common_species), function(idx) {
      sp_subset <- common_species[-idx]
      traits_subset <- traits[sp_subset, , drop = FALSE]
      comm_mat <- matrix(site_psi_mean[sp_subset], nrow = 1)
      colnames(comm_mat) <- sp_subset
      rownames(comm_mat) <- "site"

      res <- tryCatch(fd_fdiv(traits_subset, comm_mat), error = function(e) NULL)
      if (is.null(res)) {
        return(NA)
      }
      return(res$FDiv)
    })

    # Standard Jackknife CI
    obs_val <- site_results$FDiv_obs[i]
    n_sp <- length(common_species)
    jack_mean <- mean(jack_vals, na.rm = TRUE)
    jack_se <- sqrt(((n_sp - 1) / n_sp) * sum((jack_vals - jack_mean)^2, na.rm = TRUE))

    t_crit <- qt(1 - (1 - CONF_LEVEL) / 2, df = n_sp - 1)
    site_results$jack_low[i] <- obs_val - t_crit * jack_se
    site_results$jack_high[i] <- obs_val + t_crit * jack_se

    if (i %% 5 == 0) message(paste("Processed", i, "/", nrow(site_results), "sites"))
  }

  return(site_results)
}


#' Calculate group-level summaries and confidence intervals via bootstrapping
calculate_group_summary <- function(site_results) {
  group_summary <- site_results %>%
    group_by(integration_class) %>%
    summarise(
      mean_FDiv_obs = mean(FDiv_obs, na.rm = TRUE),
      n_sites = n(),
      .groups = "drop"
    )

  group_boot_stats <- site_results %>%
    filter(!is.na(FDiv_obs)) %>%
    group_by(integration_class) %>%
    do({
      data <- .$FDiv_obs
      if (length(data) > 1) {
        b_obj <- boot(data = data, statistic = function(d, i) mean(d[i], na.rm = TRUE), R = 500)
        b_ci <- tryCatch(boot.ci(b_obj, type = "perc", conf = CONF_LEVEL), error = function(e) NULL)
        if (!is.null(b_ci)) {
          data.frame(
            group_boot_low = b_ci$percent[4],
            group_boot_high = b_ci$percent[5]
          )
        } else {
          data.frame(group_boot_low = NA_real_, group_boot_high = NA_real_)
        }
      } else {
        data.frame(group_boot_low = NA_real_, group_boot_high = NA_real_)
      }
    }) %>%
    ungroup()

  group_summary <- group_summary %>%
    left_join(group_boot_stats, by = "integration_class")

  return(group_summary)
}


Next we load the data and run the functions.

Code# --- Global Configuration ---
# Number of posterior iterations to sample for site-level uncertainty
N_ITER <- 100
# Confidence level for intervals
CONF_LEVEL <- 0.95

# --- Main Execution ---


  # 1. Load Data
  data_list <- load_and_clean_data()
  traits <- data_list$traits
  site_comm <- data_list$site_comm
  sp_site_iter <- data_list$sp_site_iter

  common_species <- rownames(traits)

  # 2. Observed Functional Divergence (FDiv) using mean psi
  # message("Calculating observed FDiv...")
  comm_matrix <- site_comm %>%
    select(all_of(common_species)) %>%
    as.matrix()
  rownames(comm_matrix) <- site_comm$site_name

  obs_fdiv_df <- fd_fdiv(traits, comm_matrix) %>%
    rename(site_name = site, FDiv_obs = FDiv)

  # 3. Uncertainty Estimation (Posterior Sampling & Jackknife)
  # message("Estimating uncertainty for each site...")
  site_results <- estimate_site_uncertainty(obs_fdiv_df, traits, site_comm, sp_site_iter, common_species)

  # 4. Group-level Summary and Bootstrapping
  # message("Calculating group-level summaries...")
  group_summary_FD <- calculate_group_summary(site_results)

  # 5. Save Results
  # if (!dir.exists("out")) dir.create("out")
  # write_csv(site_results, "out/functional_diversity_results.csv")
  # write_csv(group_summary, "out/functional_diversity_groups_summary.csv")
  # message("Results saved to 'out/' directory.")

  # 6. Visualization

#' Generate plots for site and group level Functional Diversity
# generate_plots <- function(site_results, group_summary) {
#  message("Generating plots...")

  # 1. Site-level plot
  plot_data <- site_results %>%
    filter(!is.na(FDiv_obs)) %>%
    arrange(FDiv_obs) %>%
    mutate(site_name = factor(site_name, levels = site_name))

  
  p1 <- ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = site_name, y = FDiv_obs)) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = post_low, ymax = post_high, color = "Bootstrap Posterior 95% CI"),
      width = 0.3, position = position_nudge(x = -0.15)
    ) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = jack_low, ymax = jack_high, color = "Jackknife Posterior 95% CI"),
      width = 0.3, position = position_nudge(x = 0.15)
    ) +
    geom_point(aes(color = "Observed FD"), size = 1.5) +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_color_manual(values = c(
      "Observed FD" = "gray25",
      "Bootstrap Posterior 95% CI" = "#9987FF",
      "Jackknife Posterior 95% CI" = "#FF878D"
    )) +
    labs(
      title = "Functional Divergence (FDiv) by Site",
      subtitle = "From posterior samples, calculating uncertainty by Bootstrap and Jackknife",
      x = "Site Name", y = "Functional Divergence", color = "Method"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(axis.title.y = element_text(size = 8), legend.position = "right")

  print(p1)







Next we do the calculations per grouping factor.

Code  # 2. Group-level plot
  plot_data_group <- group_summary_FD %>%
    filter(!is.na(mean_FDiv_obs)) %>%
    arrange(mean_FDiv_obs) %>%
    mutate(group_factor = factor(integration_class, levels = integration_class))

datatable(plot_data_group)





and plot FD

CodeFD_t <- exp(mean(log(group_summary_FD$mean_FDiv_obs / rep(group_summary_FD$mean_FDiv_obs[4], 4) )))


  FD_plot <- ggplot(plot_data_group, aes(x = group_factor, y = mean_FDiv_obs)) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = group_boot_low, ymax = group_boot_high, color = "Bootstrap 95% CI"),
      width = 0.3
    ) +
    geom_point(aes(color = "Observed FD"), size = 2) +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_color_manual(values = c(
      "Observed FD" = "gray25",
      "Bootstrap 95% CI" = "#9987FF"
    )) +
    labs(
      title = "Mean Functional Divergence (FDiv) by Group",
      x = "Group", y = "Mean Functional Divergence", color = "Method"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")

  
FD_plot

title: Multidimensional Biodiversity Index (MBI)
text:
Structural Diversity (SD)
Shannon entropy of the foliage height class distribution, normalized by the baseline entropy so the index equals 1 at \(t = 1\).
\[SD_t = \frac{-\sum_{l=1}^{L} f_{l,t}\ln f_{l,t}}{SD_1}\]
Load Data

Code  fhd_data <- read_csv("C:/CodigoR/WildMon/FunctionalDiversityV3/data/FHD_2022_Thailand_Phase1_buffer200m_Stats.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
    rename(site_name = `site name`) %>%
    mutate(site_name = str_replace_all(site_name, " ", "_"))


Calculate by grouping factor

Code#' @param foliage_t  Proportions per height layer at time t
#' @param foliage_1  Proportions at baseline
#' @return Scalar SD value (1 at baseline)

# function to calculate SD
calc_SD <- function(foliage_t, foliage_1) {
  H <- function(p) { p <- p[p > 0]; -sum(p * log(p)) }
  H(foliage_t) / H(foliage_1)
}



Code  group_summary_SD <-  fhd_data |> 
  # melt() |> 
    group_by(integration_class) %>%
    summarise(
      mean_SD = mean(FHD_mean, na.rm = TRUE),
      #entropy_SD = entropy(FHD_mean),
      SD_SD = sd(FHD_mean, na.rm = TRUE),
      .groups = "drop"
    )



# Calculate SDt
group_summary_SD$SDt <- c (calc_SD( group_summary_SD$mean_SD[1], group_summary_SD$mean_SD[4]), 
                           calc_SD( group_summary_SD$mean_SD[2], group_summary_SD$mean_SD[4]),
                           calc_SD( group_summary_SD$mean_SD[3], group_summary_SD$mean_SD[4]),
                           calc_SD( group_summary_SD$mean_SD[4], group_summary_SD$mean_SD[4])
                           )


# Calculate SD at original table

for(i in 1:length(fhd_data$FHD_mean)){
  fhd_data$SD[i] <- calc_SD(fhd_data$FHD_mean[i], group_summary_SD$mean_SD[4])
}


see the table

Codedatatable(group_summary_SD) 





and plot SD

Code  # 2. Group-level plot
  plot_data_group3 <- group_summary_SD |> 
    # filter(!is.na(mean_FDiv_obs)) %>%
    # arrange(mean_FDiv_obs) %>%
    mutate(group_factor = factor(integration_class, levels = integration_class))

  SD_plot <- ggplot(plot_data_group3, aes(x = group_factor, y = SDt)) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = SDt-SD_SD, ymax = SDt+SD_SD, color = "Standard deviation"),
      width = 0.3
    ) +
    geom_point(aes(color = "Observed SD"), size = 2) +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_color_manual(values = c(
      "Observed SD" = "gray25",
      "Standard deviation" = "#9987FF"
    )) +
    labs(
      title = "Structural diversity by Group",
      x = "Group", y = "Structural Diversity (entropy)"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")

  
  SD_plot







Multidimensional Biodiversity Index (MBI)
\[
MBI_{t}=exp(\frac{ln TD_{t} + ln FD_{t} + ln SD_{t} }{3})
\]

CodeMBI <- cbind((mean_occupancy[,3]), 
             (mean_occupancy[,132]), # TD
             (site_results[,2]),  # FD
             (fhd_data$SD)) |> # SD 
  as.data.frame() |> 
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), round, digits = 3)) 

colnames(MBI) <- c("integration_class", "TD", "FD", "SD")

MBI$MBI <- exp((log(MBI$TD) + 
                  log(MBI$FD) + 
                  log(MBI$SD)) / 3)



  group_summary_MBI <-  MBI |> 
    mutate (integration_class=as.character(integration_class)) |> 
    mutate (TD=as.numeric(TD)) |> 
    mutate (FD=as.numeric(FD)) |> 
    mutate (SD=as.numeric(SD)) |> 
  # melt() |> 
    group_by(integration_class) %>%
    summarise(
      mean_MBI = mean(MBI, na.rm = TRUE),
      SD_MBI = sd(MBI, na.rm = TRUE),
      .groups = "drop"
    )
  



MBI_t <- exp((log(group_summary_MBI$mean_MBI[1]) + 
                log(group_summary_MBI$mean_MBI[2]) + 
                log(group_summary_MBI$mean_MBI[3])) / 3)



Code  # 2. Group-level plot
  plot_data_group3 <- group_summary_MBI |> 
    # filter(!is.na(mean_FDiv_obs)) %>%
    # arrange(mean_FDiv_obs) %>%
    mutate(group_factor = factor(integration_class, levels = integration_class))

  p4 <- ggplot(plot_data_group3, aes(x = group_factor, y = mean_MBI)) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_MBI-SD_MBI, ymax = mean_MBI+SD_MBI, color = "Standard deviation"),
      width = 0.3
    ) +
    geom_point(aes(color = "Observed MBI"), size = 2) +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_color_manual(values = c(
      "Observed MBI" = "gray25",
      "Standard deviation" = "#9987FF"
    )) +
    labs(
      title = "MBI by Group",
      x = "Group", y = "MBI"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")

  print(p4)








Codedatatable(group_summary_MBI)

title: Multidimensional Biodiversity Index (MBI)
text:
Visualization
Lets put together a summary table.

Code#| echo: true
#| code-fold: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| 


  TD  <- c((group_summary_TD$mean_TD[4]),
       mean((group_summary_TD$mean_TD[1]),  
           (group_summary_TD$mean_TD[2]),  
           (group_summary_TD$mean_TD[3])))
  
  sd_TD  <- c((group_summary_TD$SD_TD[4]),
       mean((group_summary_TD$SD_TD[1]),  
           (group_summary_TD$SD_TD[2]),  
           (group_summary_TD$SD_TD[3])))

  
  FD <- c((group_summary_FD$mean_FDiv_obs[4]),
        mean((group_summary_FD$mean_FDiv_obs[1]), 
         (group_summary_FD$mean_FDiv_obs[2]),
         (group_summary_FD$mean_FDiv_obs[3])))
  
  sd1_FD <- group_summary_FD$group_boot_high -  group_summary_FD$group_boot_low 
  
  sd_FD <- c((sd1_FD[4]),
        mean((sd1_FD[1]), 
         (sd1_FD[2]),
         (sd1_FD[3])))
  
  SD  <- c((group_summary_SD$SDt[4]),
        mean((group_summary_SD$SDt[1]), 
         (group_summary_SD$SDt[2]), 
         (group_summary_SD$SDt[3])))
  
  sd_SD  <- c((group_summary_SD$SD_SD[4]),
        mean((group_summary_SD$SD_SD[1]), 
         (group_summary_SD$SD_SD[2]), 
         (group_summary_SD$SD_SD[3])))
  
  MBI <- c((group_summary_MBI$mean_MBI[4]),
        exp((log(group_summary_MBI$mean_MBI[1]) + 
         log(group_summary_MBI$mean_MBI[2]) + 
         log(group_summary_MBI$mean_MBI[3])) / 3))
  
  sd_MBI <- c((group_summary_MBI$SD_MBI[4]),
        mean((group_summary_MBI$SD_MBI[1]), 
         (group_summary_MBI$SD_MBI[2]), 
         (group_summary_MBI$SD_MBI[3])))
  
  results <- cbind(period = 1:2, TD, sd_TD, FD, sd_FD, SD, sd_SD, MBI, sd_MBI) |> as.data.frame()
  

datatable(round(results, 4))





The first value at period 1 is the baseline. As the baseline we considered the Monoculture. The second value (period 2) is the mean of Advanced stage, Early stage and Forest.
Lets make a comparative and summary graph

Code  df_obs  <- results #%>% select(period, value = all_of(idx_name))
  # df_boot <- boot_summary %>% filter(index == idx_name)
TD_g <- ggplot(data = df_obs) +
    geom_ribbon(aes(x = period, ymin = TD - sd_TD, ymax = TD + sd_TD),
                fill = "#2196F3", alpha = 0.5) +
    geom_line(data = df_obs, aes(x = period, y = TD, group = 1),
              colour = "#2196F3", linewidth = 1.4) +
    geom_point(data = df_obs, aes(x = period, y = TD, group = 1),
               colour =  "#2196F3", size = 3.5) +
    # geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey40") +
    # scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:T, labels = paste0("t", 1:T)) +
    labs(title = "TD", x = "Survey period (baseline = Monoculture)",
         y = "Index value") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
  
FD_g <- ggplot(data = df_obs) +
    geom_ribbon(aes(x = period, ymin = FD-sd_FD, ymax = FD+sd_FD),
                fill = "#4CAF50", alpha = 0.5) +
    geom_line(data = df_obs, aes(x = period, y = FD, group = 1),
              colour = "#4CAF50", linewidth = 1.4) +
    geom_point(data = df_obs, aes(x = period, y = FD, group = 1),
               colour =  "#4CAF50", size = 3.5) +
    # geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey40") +
    # scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:T, labels = paste0("t", 1:T)) +
    labs(title = "FD", x = "Survey period (baseline = Monoculture)",
         y = "Index value ") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))


SD_g <- ggplot(data = df_obs) +
    geom_ribbon(aes(x = period, ymin = SD-sd_SD, ymax = SD+sd_SD),
                fill = "#FF9800", alpha = 0.5) +
    geom_line(data = df_obs, aes(x = period, y = SD, group = 1),
              colour = "#FF9800", linewidth = 1.4) +
    geom_point(data = df_obs, aes(x = period, y = SD, group = 1),
               colour =  "#FF9800", size = 3.5) +
    # geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey40") +
    # scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:T, labels = paste0("t", 1:T)) +
    labs(title = "SD", x = "Survey period (baseline = Monoculture)",
         y = "Index value ") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

MBI_g <- ggplot(data = df_obs) +
    geom_ribbon( aes(x = period, ymin = MBI-sd_MBI, ymax = MBI+sd_MBI),
                fill = "#9C27B0", alpha = 0.5) +
    geom_line(data = df_obs, aes(x = period, y = MBI, group = 1),
              colour = "#9C27B0", linewidth = 1.4) +
    geom_point(data = df_obs, aes(x = period, y = MBI, group = 1),
               colour =  "#9C27B0", size = 3.5) +
    # geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey40") +
    # scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:T, labels = paste0("t", 1:T)) +
    labs(title = "MBI", x = "Survey period (baseline = Monoculture)",
         y = "Index value ") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))


plot((TD_g + FD_g) / (SD_g + MBI_g))







Survey period=1 is the baseline (Monoculture). Survey period 2 is the mean of Forest, Early stage, and Advanced stage.

title: About
text:
Biodiversity indexes are mathematical measures used by ecologists to quantify the variety of life in an ecosystem.
Here we develop a new approach to calculate three biodiversity indexes that will be combined as the Multidimensional Biodiversity Index (MBI) at the end.
A composite biodiversity index is a multi-layered metric designed to aggregate diverse datasets—often spanning different taxa, ecosystems, or ecological dimensions—into a single summary trend to aid decision-makers and communicate complex biological changes.
Because biodiversity is a broad concept encompassing species, genetic, and ecosystem diversity, it is difficult to capture holistically in any single indicator.


The primary goal of a composite index is to determine the rate of biodiversity change over time for specific regions or species groups.
A high-quality biodiversity index should meet several technical criteria:

Sensitivity to Key Components: It should decrease if overall abundance, species richness, or species evenness (the similarity in abundance among different species) decreases.
Independence from Sample Size: The expected value should not be a function of the sample size.
Precision: It should have a measurable and reliable level of precision.
Handling Dominance: It should ideally reduce the influence of a single dominant species to prevent it from masking trends on rarer species.

title: About
text:
Biodiversity indexes are mathematical measures used by ecologists to quantify the variety of life in an ecosystem.
Here we develop a new approach to calculate three biodiversity indexes that will be combined as the Multidimensional Biodiversity Index (MBI) at the end.
A composite biodiversity index is a multi-layered metric designed to aggregate diverse datasets—often spanning different taxa, ecosystems, or ecological dimensions—into a single summary trend to aid decision-makers and communicate complex biological changes.
Because biodiversity is a broad concept encompassing species, genetic, and ecosystem diversity, it is difficult to capture holistically in any single indicator.


The primary goal of a composite index is to determine the rate of biodiversity change over time for specific regions or species groups.
A high-quality biodiversity index should meet several technical criteria:

Sensitivity to Key Components: It should decrease if overall abundance, species richness, or species evenness (the similarity in abundance among different species) decreases.
Independence from Sample Size: The expected value should not be a function of the sample size.
Precision: It should have a measurable and reliable level of precision.
Handling Dominance: It should ideally reduce the influence of a single dominant species to prevent it from masking trends on rarer species.

title: About
section: What Should a Good Biodiversity Indicator Look Like?
text:
What Should a Good Biodiversity Indicator Look Like?
Measure diversity at various levels:

Taxonomic diversity (not just species richness)
Functional diversity (the “jobs” that species do)
Structural diversity (habitat complexity)

title: About
text:
Technical Formulations and Dimensions
A robust composite biodiversity indicator may integrate information across different ecological levels. Here we propose a conceptual formulation. The Multidimensional Biodiversity Index (MBI), combines three primary dimensions:
Taxonomic Diversity (TD): Accounts for species richness and relative abundances, often using the geometric mean of abundances to ensure proportional sensitivity.
Functional Diversity (FD): Measures the diversity of ecological roles or “jobs” species perform, which is linked to ecosystem resilience and services.
Structural Diversity (SD): Reflects physical habitat complexity, such as canopy height variation in forests, often quantified via LiDAR.

Taxonomic Diversity (geometric mean of occupancy)
\[
TD_{t}=exp(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i}^{} ln \frac{d_{i,t}}{d_{i,1}})
\] were: - n = number of species - di,t = abundance of species i at time t - di,1 = abundance of species i at time 1


Functional Diversity (traits/jobs)
Rooted at 1 which allows to evaluate changes over time Can be generalized to n traits (continuous and discrete)
\[
FD_{t}=\frac{\sum_{i}^{}z_{i,t}a_{i,t}}{\sum_{i}^{}z_{i,1}a_{i,1}}
\] were: - zi,t = distance to centroid for species i at time t - ai,1 = relative abundance of species i at time 1 (also occupancy)


Structural Diversity (Foliage height diversity)
Rooted at 1 which allows to evaluate changes over time
\[
SD_{t}=-\sum_{i=1}^{n}f_{i,t}ln f_{i,t}/SD_{1}
\] were: - SD = Foliage height diversity at time t - fi,t = proportion of foliage at heigh at time t

title: About
text:
Multidimensional Biodiversity Index (MBI)
\[
MBI_{t}=exp(\frac{ln TD_{t} + ln FD_{t} + ln SD_{t} }{3})
\]






Notewere the results of MIB indicate:




MBI > 0 Increasing.

MBI < 0 Decreasing.

MBI = 0 Unchanged.
